title: Quiz: Natural Gradients
description: Test your understanding of quantum natural gradients

questions.question: What is the natural gradient formula?
questions.explanation: The natural gradient is g̃ = F⁻¹g, where F is the Fisher information matrix (or Fubini-Study metric tensor) and g is the vanilla gradient. This accounts for the geometry of the parameter space.

questions.question: What does the Fubini-Study metric measure?
questions.explanation: The Fubini-Study metric measures the 'distance' or distinguishability between quantum states. It captures how much the quantum state changes with respect to parameter changes.

questions.question: How many circuit evaluations are needed for the full metric tensor with n parameters?
questions.explanation: Computing the full n×n Fubini-Study metric tensor requires O(n²) circuit evaluations since each element F_ij requires multiple circuit runs.

questions.question: Natural gradients always converge faster than vanilla gradients.
questions.explanation: False. While natural gradients often converge faster for ill-conditioned problems, the computational overhead of computing the metric may outweigh benefits. Also, for well-conditioned problems, vanilla gradients can be equally effective.

questions.question: What is a common approximation to reduce natural gradient cost?
questions.explanation: Block-diagonal approximation assumes the metric is block-diagonal (e.g., per layer), reducing cost from O(n²) to O(L×k²) where L is layers and k is parameters per layer.

questions.question: Why is regularization needed when computing natural gradients?

options.text: To make the metric matrix invertible and numerically stable
To reduce the number of parameters

questions.explanation: Regularization (F + λI) is needed because the metric matrix may be singular or nearly singular, making F⁻¹ numerically unstable. Adding λI ensures invertibility.

questions.question: For which type of optimization landscape are natural gradients most beneficial?

options.text: Isotropic landscapes (equal sensitivity in all directions)

questions.explanation: Natural gradients excel for highly anisotropic landscapes where different parameters have vastly different sensitivities. They normalize the update step across parameter dimensions.

questions.question: Which are valid ways to compute the Fubini-Study metric? (Select all)

options.text: From the Hamiltonian directly without circuits

questions.explanation: The metric can be computed using parameter-shift circuits (a) which work on hardware, or classical simulation of wavefunction derivatives (b) in simulators. Random sampling doesn't compute the metric, and you need circuit evaluations.
